note_data.title: Forces and equilibrium
keyPoints: A particle is in **equilibrium** when the resultant of all forces acting on it is zero
The **weight** of an object acts vertically downward from the centre of gravity
**Resolve** forces into perpendicular components using trigonometry: F cos θ and F sin θ
The **normal reaction** acts perpendicular to the surface and away from the surface
**Friction** acts parallel to the surface and opposes motion or tendency to move
For equilibrium on an inclined plane: resolve parallel and perpendicular to the plane
Maximum static friction is F = μR where μ is coefficient of friction and R is normal reaction
When forces are in equilibrium: ΣFx = 0 and ΣFy = 0 in any two perpendicular directions

definitions.definition: The state when the resultant of all forces acting on a particle is zero
The contact force exerted by a surface on an object, acting perpendicular to the surface
The force that acts parallel to surfaces in contact and opposes motion or tendency to move
The ratio of the maximum frictional force to the normal reaction between two surfaces

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Show clear working with correct units in final answer
Work towards the given answer step by step

modelAnswer.question: A particle of weight 50 N rests in equilibrium on a smooth inclined plane at 30° to the horizontal, supported by a string parallel to the plane. Find the tension in the string and the normal reaction.
modelAnswer.answer:
Draw diagram showing weight, tension T up the plane, normal reaction R perpendicular to plane.
Resolve parallel to plane: T = 50 sin 30° = 50 × 0.5 = 25 N
Resolve perpendicular to plane: R = 50 cos 30° = 50 × (√3/2) = 25√3 N
Therefore tension = 25 N and normal reaction = 25√3 N
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct diagram with forces labelled
Mark 4: Both correct answers with units

commonMistakes: Never resolve weight as mg sin θ down the plane and mg cos θ into the plane without stating which direction is positive
Students often forget to draw a clear force diagram before attempting calculations
Confusing the angle: if plane is at θ to horizontal, component down plane is mg sin θ, not mg cos θ
Forgetting that normal reaction acts away from the surface, not into it
Writing friction = μR even when no friction exists (smooth surfaces)
examinerTips: Always draw a labelled diagram showing all forces for full marks
This topic appears in almost every Paper 4 mechanics section
State clearly which direction you are taking as positive before resolving
Check your trigonometry: down the slope uses sin, perpendicular to slope uses cos
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Force diagram showing weight acting vertically downward, normal reaction perpendicular to inclined surface, tension/friction parallel to surface, with angle θ marked between incline and horizontal
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate the tension in a cable supporting a sign at equilibrium and explain why three forces were needed for stability
N/D 2022 P1 Q8 (3 marks) — candidates had to identify which diagram showed forces in equilibrium and state the principle of moments
M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — students calculated unknown forces in a beam balanced on a pivot using moments, including drawing a force diagram
N/D 2021 P2 Q6 (4 marks) — question required explaining why a ladder against a wall needs friction forces and calculating the minimum coefficient of friction

workedExample.question: A uniform beam of length 4.0 m and weight 50 N is supported at its center. A load of 30 N is placed 1.5 m from the left end. Calculate the force needed at the right end to keep the beam horizontal. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Draw a diagram showing all forces and distances from the pivot (center of beam at 2.0 m)
Step 2: Identify clockwise moments: 30 N × (2.0 - 1.5) m = 30 × 0.5 = 15 N⋅m
Step 3: For equilibrium, anticlockwise moments = clockwise moments
Step 4: Force F × 2.0 m = 15 N⋅m, therefore F = 15 ÷ 2.0 = 7.5 N downward
workedExample.answer: The force needed at the right end is 7.5 N downward to maintain equilibrium
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct diagram, 1 mark for calculating clockwise moment, 1 mark for applying principle of moments, 1 mark for correct final answer with unit

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to draw arrows showing force directions clearly' — always use clear arrows and label forces with magnitude and direction when drawing force diagrams
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often forgot that weight acts at the center of mass' — always show the weight of an object acting downward from its center, not from pivot points or supports
Examiner Report 2021: 'Common error was not stating that sum of clockwise moments equals sum of anticlockwise moments' — always write this principle explicitly before calculating in moments problems
note_data.keyDiagram: Essential force diagram showing: object drawn as simple shape, all forces as labeled arrows with magnitudes, clear pivot point marked with triangle symbol, perpendicular distances from pivot to force lines shown as dotted lines, and distances labeled with measurements. Must include weight acting downward from center of mass.
note_data.quickRecap: For equilibrium: resultant force = 0 in all directions, and clockwise moments = anticlockwise moments about any point. Always draw clear force diagrams with labeled arrows. Weight acts from center of mass, and moment = force × perpendicular distance from pivot. Check both force balance and moment balance for complete equilibrium.
